sections.title: What is verb–subject agreement?
sections.content:
The **verb** must match the **subject** in number:

• **Singular subject** → singular verb: The cat **is** here. He **works** every day.
• **Plural subject** → plural verb: The cats **are** here. They **work** every day.

Wrong: *The cat are here.* *They works.* Right: The cat **is**; They **work**.

• **Check** – test your understanding.
• **Practice** – correct agreement errors.

sections.title: Third person singular: the -s
sections.content:
In the **present simple**, when the subject is **third person singular** (he, she, it, or one person/thing), we add **-s** (or -es) to the verb:

• I/you/we/they **have** → he/she/it **has**
• I/you/we/they **go** → he/she/it **goes**
• I/you/we/they **do** → he/she/it **does**
• I/you/we/they **are** → he/she/it **is**

Wrong: *He have been.* *She don't know.* *It go wrong.*
Right: He **has** been. She **doesn't** know. It **goes** wrong.

**Have** and **do** are irregular: he/she/it **has**, **does** (not haves, do).

sections.title: Don't be fooled by words in between
sections.content:
The verb agrees with the **subject**, not with a noun that comes later in the sentence:

• **The list** of items **is** on the table. (subject = list, singular)
• **One** of the students **was** late. (subject = one)
• **The people** in the room **are** waiting. (subject = people, plural)

Wrong: *The list of items are on the table.* Right: The list **is**.

sections.title: Compound subjects
sections.content:
• **A and B** (two subjects joined by *and*) → usually **plural**: Tom and Anna **are** friends.
• **A or B** / **Neither A nor B** → verb agrees with the **nearest** subject: Neither the teacher nor the students **were** there. (nearest = students, plural)
• **Each** / **Every** → singular: Each of us **has** a copy. Every student **takes** the test.

sections.title: Quick summary
sections.content:
• Singular subject → singular verb; plural subject → plural verb.
• **Third person singular** (he/she/it): present simple adds **-s** (has, goes, does, is).
• Wrong: *He have been* → Right: He **has** been.
• Find the real subject; ignore words in between (The list of items **is**).
• And = plural; or/nor = agree with nearest; each/every = singular.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Third person singular: he/she/it takes has (not have). He has been ill.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Third person singular: she + present simple = doesn't like (do → does; like stays without -s after does).

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: The list of items are on the table.
options.b: The list of items is on the table.
options.c: The list of items be on the table.
options.d: The list of items were on the table.

questions.explanation: Subject is 'the list' (singular), not 'items'. The list is on the table.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: Each of the students have a book.
options.b: Each of the students has a book.
options.c: Each of the students having a book.
options.d: Each of the students are having a book.

questions.explanation: Each is singular, so the verb is singular: each has a book.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Two subjects with 'and' = plural. Tom and Anna are late.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He have been to Paris.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Third person singular: he has (not have).

questions.question: Wrong: 'She don't know the answer.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She does not know the answer.
She does not know the answer
questions.explanation: Third person singular: she doesn't (do → does).

questions.question: Wrong: 'The book on the shelves are old.' Correct the sentence.
answers: The book on the shelves is old.
The book on the shelves is old
questions.explanation: Subject is 'the book' (singular). The book is old.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Everyone have their own copy.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Everyone has his or her own copy.
Everyone has his or her own copy

questions.question: Wrong: 'He go to work by bus.' Correct the sentence.
answers: He goes to work by bus.
He goes to work by bus
questions.explanation: Third person singular present simple: he goes (add -es).

questions.question: Wrong: 'Neither the teacher nor the students was there.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Neither the teacher nor the students were there.
Neither the teacher nor the students were there
questions.explanation: With nor, verb agrees with the nearest subject (students = plural). Were.

questions.question: Wrong: 'One of the windows are broken.' Correct the sentence.
answers: One of the windows is broken.
One of the windows is broken

questions.question: Wrong: 'It don't matter.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Third person singular: it doesn't (do → does).

questions.question: Wrong: 'My brother have a car.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Third person singular: my brother = he → has.

questions.question: Wrong: 'The news were bad.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: News is uncountable and takes a singular verb: the news was/is bad.
